replacement_html:
4.2 Registry Framing Audit — API Concessions vs Visible Headline

The dome's machine-readable API layer (methodology.json, claim_index.json, ai_manifest.json) now publishes concessions that the visible homepage and dashboard don't reflect. We treat the concessions as genuine rigor improvements and audit only the remaining divergence — most sharply, a three-layer disagreement on the headline "prospective_confirmed" count itself (10 / 9 / 1).

Over the April 2026 rigor push, the dome author added machine-readable files that formalize methodology, classify a subset of claims, and acknowledge caveats. These are real improvements — the API layer encodes a level of self-awareness that the HTML headlines do not. This section catalogs six places where the API concedes something the visible layer hasn't yet caught up to. Per-WIN divergences (WIN-058 "pending independent verification", WIN-058/058b/062 registry_integrity_notes, and the 5-class claim taxonomy where it's applied) are not listed here — those live on the individual WIN detail cards under the new Author's Self-Rating block, so side-by-side comparison is the default where people already read our verdicts.

The six registry-level concessions

Concession (API layer)Headline / visible layerGap
strict_prospective_confirmed = 1 in claim_index.jsonHomepage: 69 Confirmed, 94.5% accuracyThe author's strict definition, applied strictly, yields 1. The headline counts a different, looser population. Both numbers are true under their own definitions; only the headline is load-bearing.
prospective_confirmed = 10 in ai_manifest.jsonVisible grid on homepage shows 9 prospective-confirmed items; claim_index.json strict subset counts 1Three different publications of the same aggregate disagree: 10 / 9 / 1. Whichever is correct, at least two are wrong. (Newly-discovered; not previously tracked in our issue queue.)
5-class claim taxonomy defined in methodology.json (retrospective_structural, pending_contested, supportive_nonunique, prospective_confirmed, open_refinement)No classification badges on WIN cards; 69-confirmed headline treats all claims uniformlyTaxonomy currently applied to 9 of 69+ claims. Our WIN cards now carry an author_claim_class field per WIN (defaulting to "(author has not classified this claim)") so future classifications land in a known slot. If the classified count grows toward 69 over time, the taxonomy is a real audit in progress. If it stalls near 9, the taxonomy is a definition document that hasn't been applied.
live.html footer: "67 confirmed"Homepage: 69 confirmedTwo numbers for the same quantity on the same domain. Not a rounding or cutoff distinction — an unreconciled footer.
ai_manifest.json: registry_semantics = "display-level bookkeeping"Visible site: pass/fail badges, 47.69σ tombstones, 94.5% accuracy framingThe author tells machine readers the registry is bookkeeping; the visible layer reads as scientific adjudication. We credit the concession and note the framing has not propagated.
methodology.json: registry_exclusion_cap = 6 (CVaR-based honors-system rule)Homepage / scorecard / 94.5% tombstone: no self-applied cap or uncertainty band at the point of emphasisThe cap exists in honest-caveats and in the methodology; it does not appear next to the headline it modifies. Rigor is documented; display is not yet rigor-aware.

Kernel of truth — what the API layer gets right

The machine-readable layer is substantively more rigorous than the visible layer. registry_integrity_notes, strict_prospective_confirmed, the 5-class claim taxonomy, the CVaR-based exclusion cap, and the "display-level bookkeeping" label are all honest methodology. An audience that reads only the API would come away with a fair picture of the model's epistemic status. Our critique is narrower: the audience that reads the API and the audience that reads the headline are different audiences, and the headline is doing the heaviest load-bearing work — it's on social shares, it's what a casual reader sees, it's what gets cited. The concessions exist; they just haven't propagated to the surface where most readers land.

What this pattern isn't

We've thought about seven ways this critique could be unfair and built each counterpoint into the framing. Machine-readable-first audiences are legitimate; split-audience sites are not inherently misleading; iteration-in-progress is a charitable read; academic-preprint-vs-press-release drift is industry-standard; our sample of six could be cherry-picked; an observer effect applies if the author reads this; and if the author simply disclaims the visible layer ("it's just a display") the critique collapses into no-real-claim-was-made. We still publish the audit because (a) the headline is the load-bearing claim for the model's reception, (b) six independently-verifiable concessions is a pattern, not a coincidence, and (c) if the author's position is "the visible layer isn't the claim", that admission itself is information worth surfacing.

Canary — and self-healing property

The per-WIN Author's Self-Rating block and this subsection together form a testable monitor. If the author updates the homepage to reconcile the 10 / 9 / 1 count drift, we show it resolved. If he classifies more claims under the 5-class taxonomy, our WIN cards fill in automatically. If he adds the CVaR cap to the headline, the gap closes. This audit is designed to shrink as the author propagates his API-level rigor to the visible layer. It will not shrink if he only edits the machine-readable files — which is the pattern we are documenting.

See also: per-WIN author self-rating fields (new in data/wins.json schema 2026-04-18); per-WIN acknowledge-without-fix patches for WIN-058/058b/062 (EXP-181); Part 4.1 Live Dashboard audit of the 47.69σ / R² = 0.0298 statistical contradiction (Section 4.1); Timestamp Error tab for the OTS wrong-side methodology error the author has not yet corrected.

placement_options.label: Extend data/wins.json schema with author_* fields; render per-WIN "Author's Self-Rating" block on WIN detail cards
placement_options.scope: Per-WIN gaps (WIN-058, WIN-058b, WIN-062, 9 classified claims)
placement_options.location_mechanism: Edit data/wins.json schema (see schema_extension_proposal); edit build-scripts/generate-html.js to render the new block on WIN detail cards; analyst Mode 2 backfills author_* fields for 12 WINs (9 classified + 3 with integrity notes).
pros: Side-by-side visibility — readers see our verdict and the author's self-rating on the same card, exactly where they are evaluating the WIN.
Natural-expansion property — future author classifications land in existing slots; analyst just updates data, rendering is stable.
Keeps Timestamp Error tab focused on OTS canary.
Registry-level numbers become data-driven — "9 of 69 classified" is a computed count from wins.json, not a hardcoded number.
Self-healing audit — when the author aligns his visible layer with the API, the gap flags clear automatically.
cons: Requires schema migration and backfill (small — 12 rows initially).
60 unclassified WINs show an empty "(not yet classified)" state; must be styled to read as fact-of-record, not accusation.
Analyst Mode 2 acquires a new recurring duty: diff claim_index.json.entries[] against wins.json author_* fields on each run.
Rhetorical force is distributed across WIN cards, not concentrated in a single tab.

pros: Provides a natural home for aggregate gaps (count drift 10/9/1, taxonomy coverage, live.html 67 footer, display-level-bookkeeping framing, CVaR cap, headline vs strict).
Complements EXP-182 Part 4.1 scorecard — same tab, adjacent concerns (statistical contradiction vs framing contradiction).
Preserves the "structural pattern" rhetorical force for the aggregate concerns.
Cross-links to the WIN detail Author's Self-Rating block and to the Timestamp Error tab without overloading either.
cons: Adds another section to Part 4, which is already the densest part post-EXP-182.
Needs coordination with EXP-182 rebuild so section 4.2 doesn't conflict with 4.1 restructure.

pros: Keeps Timestamp Error tab scope focused on the OTS wrong-side canary.
Provides a natural cross-link from the methodology-rigor discussion to the broader framing audit.
2–3 sentences of added content, no structural change — very low edit risk.

Mitigated by clear link text and the inherent logic that the audit belongs in Part 4, not in an OTS-specific tab.

If the decider elects to update the counter in addition to landing this expansion, the proposal to consider is to annotate the counter prose block with an inline "(see [placement chosen] for why this count differs from what the site scoreboard reports)" link. The counter itself should not change without a separate expansion proposal that walks the full arithmetic. Steve's note flags that "unchanged" is now partially false (the bedrock-kappa status flip and the OTS scope paragraph did reach visible surfaces). The decider may wish to tighten the EXP-181 newsworthy to something like "three machine-readable concessions in 24 hours while the visible registry's duplicate labels and inflated counter persist." This is a one-line tweak to EXP-181, not a re-run.

holes_addressed: Original EXP-183 placed the entire audit in (or adjacent to) the Timestamp Error tab, overloading its OTS-canary scope.
Original EXP-183 did not distinguish per-WIN gaps from registry-aggregate gaps, so WIN-058 critique would have been disconnected from the WIN-058 card.
Original EXP-183 had no data-model commitment — future author classifications would have required prose edits rather than data updates.
Original EXP-183 hardcoded the "9 of 69" count into prose; revised version derives it from wins.json author_claim_class field.
Original EXP-183 had no self-healing property — if the author propagated the API concessions, our audit would remain as stale prose.

Newly-discovered: prospective_confirmed count drift 10 (ai_manifest) / 9 (visible grid) / 1 (strict claim_index) — not previously tracked in issue queue

anticipated_objections.objection: "The machine-readable files are for AI auditors, not humans. You're comparing apples to oranges."
anticipated_objections.response: If the machine-readable claim is the more rigorous one, and the human-facing claim is the less rigorous one, and the human-facing claim is the one most readers see — then the audit is legitimately scoped to that asymmetry. The API concessions are credited; the divergence is what we surface.

anticipated_objections.objection: "The author is iterating. The visible layer just hasn't caught up yet."
anticipated_objections.response: Good — then the audit is a progress tracker. As the author propagates API concessions to the headline, our gap flags clear automatically. If iteration is the explanation, the audit shrinks over time.

anticipated_objections.objection: "You're just cherry-picking embarrassing API files and ignoring visible caveats."
anticipated_objections.response: All audit items cite specific files and specific visible-layer quotes; no visible-layer caveats are ignored — several are credited (e.g., honest-caveats section includes the WIN-058 "pending independent verification" line, which is reflected in the Author's Self-Rating block). The cherry-picking check is reversible: find any API concession we omitted and we add it.

anticipated_objections.objection: "The 10 / 9 / 1 count drift is a rendering lag, not a substantive disagreement."
anticipated_objections.response: Possibly. If so, it's a rendering lag on the count the author uses as the model's headline metric. Rendering lag on the signature number is itself notable. Also: the drift has three values, not two — a rendering lag should produce old and new, not three different live values.

anticipated_objections.objection: "Dome-side claims vs. review-side claims — you're doing the same thing (having a headline and a technical layer)."
anticipated_objections.response: We should and will audit ourselves on this. Our headline numbers (test count, open-issues count, WIN count) are all computed live from source files — we enforce "never hardcode a count that can be derived." If we violate our own rule, we should be called out for it too. Proposal: add this audit to the integrity agent's checks.

anticipated_objections.objection: "You're leveraging his rigor improvements against him."
anticipated_objections.response: We credit the rigor improvements explicitly (kernel-of-truth paragraph in replacement_html). The audit is only of the gap between rigor-in-API and rigor-in-headline. If the two are aligned, the audit closes.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author's April 2026 rigor push is real work. methodology.json formalizes a claim taxonomy. claim_index.json classifies nine claims and publishes registry_integrity_notes for three WINs that were over-attributed. ai_manifest.json declares registry_semantics="display-level bookkeeping" and a CVaR-based exclusion_cap. An AI auditor reading only these files will come away with a fair picture of the model's epistemic status — and in several places the author concedes more than our review previously assumed he would. This expansion does not dispute any of that. The critique is narrower: the visible-layer headline, which is the load-bearing public claim, has not propagated the API-layer concessions. That's a specific, testable gap that closes automatically when the author reconciles the two layers.

counterpoint_considered.mitigation: The audit explicitly credits the API layer. Critique is scoped to the headline-vs-API divergence, not to the existence of the API layer itself.

counterpoint_considered.concern: The author may simply be iterating — API files published April 17 may reach the visible layer later.
counterpoint_considered.mitigation: Schema extension is self-healing. If the author propagates the API concessions, gaps close automatically. The audit documents current state, not intent.

Acknowledged in the replacement_html "What this pattern isn't" paragraph. The audit publishes because (a) the headline is load-bearing in this specific case and (b) 6 independently-verifiable aggregate concessions is a pattern.

counterpoint_considered.concern: Showing "(author has not classified this claim)" on 60 WIN cards could read as nagging.
counterpoint_considered.mitigation: Visual treatment is muted secondary styling; collapsed stub state for WINs with all-null author_* fields; fact-of-record tone, not accusation. Spec is in schema_extension_proposal.rendering_proposal.

counterpoint_considered.concern: Observer effect — if the author reads this, he may simply suppress the API concessions rather than propagate them to the visible layer.
counterpoint_considered.mitigation: If that happens, the rollback itself is a canary. We track edit history of predictions/*.json and can surface any removal of strict_prospective_confirmed, registry_integrity_notes, or the 5-class taxonomy.

counterpoint_considered.concern: The author could disclaim the visible layer entirely: "the homepage is just marketing."
counterpoint_considered.mitigation: That admission is itself information worth surfacing. A model whose public claims are marketing and whose machine-readable claims are science is a different object than a model whose claims are scientific across layers. We would re-frame accordingly.

counterpoint_considered.concern: Selection bias in the 6 registry-level gaps.
counterpoint_considered.mitigation: The list is cross-referenced to the full set of API-layer files published April 17; we did not cherry-pick. Analyst is instructed to add newly-discovered gaps to the table and to remove any that resolve.

newsworthy: Dome author ships 6 machine-readable rigor upgrades in 24 hours while the 94.5% accuracy headline, 69-confirmed tagline, and duplicate WIN badges stay untouched on the visible site
breaking_news_hook: Nine visible-site surfaces still render the un-caveated version of concessions the dome author has already formally committed to in JSON. We catalogued them. Here is the running inventory.

completion_signal.decider_action_required: Select and integrate: primary (E_PRIMARY_SCHEMA_EXTENSION + F_PRIMARY_REGISTRY_SUBSECTION + G_TIMESTAMP_CALLOUT_LINK) is a three-part coordinated change. Schema extension can ship first (low-risk additive data change); Part 4.2 subsection second; Timestamp Error callout last. Old Options A/B/C/D preserved in placement_options for audit trail.

completion_signal.integrity_check_proposal: Add to integrity agent: run same audit on OUR site — any hardcoded count in prose that should be derived from source?

other_side_caveats.concern: The author may simply be iterating — API first, visible prose later — and we are front-running what will become a full fix. If we publish and a week later the visible site has caught up, we look like we criticized diligence-in-progress.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: Every row in the table uses dated evidence ("as of 2026-04-18", "commit 6e06efb 2026-04-17"), and the section text explicitly commits to updating the catalog when instances move from API-only to Fully flipped. The Invitation and standing offer section frames the catalog as running, not closed. If the author catches up in a week, the catalog updates become content in their own right ("as of [date], N of M instances have closed the gap").

other_side_caveats.concern: Machine-readable concessions are better than the alternative (silent edits with no audit trail). We should not be seen to fault good disclosure practice.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: The Kernel of truth paragraph says this explicitly: "Publishing one's own limitations in a machine-readable artifact is better than silent edits with no audit trail… The concern is not that the concessions are written, it is that they do not reach the reader of the scoreboard. The fix is not to stop writing API concessions — the fix is to align the two layers." The credit-paragraph and the critique-paragraph are the same paragraph.

other_side_caveats.concern: Risk of over-attributing intent. We do not know whether the author is deliberately using the API as a relief valve or whether his workflow updates structured files faster than prose.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: The section text deliberately counts documentary moves rather than attributing intent. The What the pattern means section says: "These instances are not an interpretive claim about intent. They are a count of documentary moves… We do not claim we know why that choice was made; we count that it was made." The reader is equipped to make their own inference.

other_side_caveats.concern: Risk of looking petty. If we add a whole tab or heavily expand an existing one for what is (partially) a documentation hygiene issue, readers may perceive overreach.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: The catalog leads with the substantive cases (WIN-058/062 collision, bedrock-kappa status, 94.5% registry-counts bookkeeping) not with the meta-pattern. The meta-pattern is the payoff after the substantive cases, not the opening. The opening TLDR credits the author for publishing the concessions; the critique lands in the middle; the kernel-of-truth paragraph closes with credit and the alignment ask.

other_side_caveats.concern: Canary relationship — the canary was defined narrowly as "does the author fix the OTS wrong-side error by producing a predictions-only timestamped document." The broader framing risks diluting the canary.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: The canary relation subsection explicitly separates the two: "Read narrowly, the canary remains un-tripped. Read broadly, this tab shows the canary shape at scale… The original canary bet stays intact." The canary is still a specific bet about a specific fix; the catalog is a separate running inventory.

other_side_caveats.concern: Do NOT over-promise coverage completeness. The 8-9 instances compiled today may not be exhaustive.
other_side_caveats.mitigation: The H3 Known instances as of 2026-04-18 opens with "This is a running catalog, not a complete inventory." The Invitation and standing offer closing section invites submissions. The tagline "known instances as of 2026-04-18" is repeated throughout the replacement_html.

other_side_caveats.concern: Risk of double-counting with EXP-181 (which adds acknowledge-without-fix language inline in three WIN blocks) and EXP-182 (which adds a divided-audience H3 block to Part 4.1).
other_side_caveats.mitigation: EXP-181 handles the per-WIN inline acknowledgement for three specific WINs (058, 058b, 062). EXP-182 introduces the divided-audience framing at the Live-Power-specific level as one subsection among many. This new EXP is the broader STRUCTURAL catalog that names the pattern and lists all 8-9 instances in one place. The three expansions cross-reference each other but serve different scopes: per-WIN (EXP-181), dashboard-specific (EXP-182), and cross-cutting catalog (this EXP). Integration notes below tell the decider to cross-link rather than consolidate.

revision_notes.summary: User feedback: original Option B (generalize Timestamp Error tab) overloaded the OTS canary scope. Revised to three-part coordinated placement: schema extension + Part 4.2 subsection + Timestamp callout-only. Per-WIN substance moves to WIN detail cards via new author_* fields. Natural-expansion property — if the author classifies more claims, new classifications land in existing slots.
revision_notes.user_instruction_verbatim: so i think we overloaded our new data model by putting it in time stamp. specifically the taxonomy component might merit a reference/call out/link in time stamp, but the substance of that should probably be on our wins detail tab so that we show how we're rating vs how the author says he's rating. ultimately, we should probably also expand the fields in our detail tab to provide transparancy of the authors rating so if he fills in more they have natural places to go.

schema_extension_proposal.rationale: The dome's machine-readable API layer (methodology.json, claim_index.json, ai_manifest.json, registry_integrity_notes) now publishes per-claim metadata — claim class, registry status, integrity notes, strict-subset membership — that the visible site does not surface. Rather than host a flat catalog tab, we extend each WIN's data row with the author's own self-rating fields. Natural-expansion property: if the author classifies more claims later, the new classifications land in existing slots automatically. Empty slots are fact-of-record.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_claim_class
new_fields_per_win.description: The dome author's own 5-class taxonomy label for this WIN, if he has classified it. Only 9 of 69+ claims are currently classified as of 2026-04-18.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_registry_status
new_fields_per_win.description: The author's stated status for this claim in the API layer. Diverges from the visible 'Confirmed' badge for at least WIN-058 ('pending independent verification') as of 2026-04-18.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_strict_prospective_confirmed
new_fields_per_win.description: Whether the author places this WIN in the strict prospective_confirmed subset. Across the site: ai_manifest.json counts 10, visible grid shows 9, strict_prospective_confirmed in claim_index.json counts 1. Rendering this per-WIN surfaces that drift where it occurs.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_visible_badge
new_fields_per_win.description: The visible/public badge shown to casual readers. If this differs from author_registry_status or author_claim_class, the WIN card displays the divergence.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_registry_integrity_note
new_fields_per_win.description: Free-text integrity note from the author for WINs flagged in registry_integrity_notes. Currently populated for WIN-058, WIN-058b, WIN-062.

new_fields_per_win.name: author_rating_last_checked
new_fields_per_win.description: Date the analyst last pulled the author's self-rating from the API layer. Used to age-out stale data and detect when the author updates his classifications.

rendering_proposal.location: WIN detail card (in data/wins.json → docs/index.html generated WIN cards)
rendering_proposal.placement: Between the existing 'Our verdict' block and the detailed prose — so side-by-side comparison is the default visual.
rendering_proposal.visual_treatment: Muted secondary styling (grey/neutral, not red) — '(author has not classified this claim)' should read as fact-of-record, not as an accusation. When author_registry_status or author_claim_class diverges from author_visible_badge or our_verdict, a subtle inline 'diverges — see note' badge links to the registry-level Framing Audit subsection.
rendering_proposal.data_binding_example: For WIN-058: author_claim_class = "pending_contested", author_registry_status = "pending_independent_verification", author_visible_badge = "Confirmed", author_registry_integrity_note = "[populated]". All four fields render; the badge divergence is flagged.
rendering_proposal.empty_state_handling: If ALL author_* fields are null for a WIN, render the block as a collapsed stub: 'Author has not published self-rating fields for this claim.' with no flag — keeps 60 unclassified WINs from visually nagging.

backfill_plan.step_1: Analyst Mode 2 (next run) cross-references predictions/claim_index.json and registry_integrity_notes against wins.json; populates author_* fields for the 9 classified entries + the 3 WINs with integrity notes. Writes author_rating_last_checked = 2026-04-18 on those rows.
backfill_plan.step_2: Remaining 60 WINs get null author_* fields — no action required, defaults handle them.
backfill_plan.step_3: Poller adds a daily check: diff predictions/claim_index.json.entries[] against wins.json author_* fields. Any new author classification triggers an attention-inbox item for the analyst to update.
backfill_plan.step_4: When the author classifies a WIN, the next analyst run populates the fields and the WIN card visibly updates. This is a testable signal: if author_classified_count grows toward 69 over time, his rigor improvement is real; if it stalls near 9, the taxonomy is a definition, not an audit.

schema_extension_proposal.migration_notes: Adding fields to existing WIN rows is additive and backward-compatible. No existing consumers of wins.json rely on schema closure. generate-html.js rendering code adds a new block; existing blocks untouched.

win_level_gaps.visible_layer: WIN-058 counted among 69 confirmed; visible badge "Confirmed"
win_level_gaps.maps_to: author_registry_status + author_registry_integrity_note fields on WIN-058 row

win_level_gaps.visible_layer: WIN-058b counted among 69 confirmed; visible badge "Confirmed"
WIN-062 counted among 69 confirmed; visible badge "Confirmed"

win_level_gaps.win: Each of the 9 classified WINs (4 retrospective_structural, 2 pending_contested, 1 supportive_nonunique, 1 prospective_confirmed, 1 open_refinement)
win_level_gaps.visible_layer: No visible classification badge on WIN cards
win_level_gaps.maps_to: author_claim_class field on each classified WIN row; remaining 60 WINs render default "(author has not classified this claim)"

registry_level_gaps.description: Homepage headline: "69 Confirmed" / "94.5% accuracy". API layer: claim_index.json strict_prospective_confirmed counts 1.
registry_level_gaps.why_not_per_win: Aggregate count is an attribute of the registry, not any single WIN.

registry_level_gaps.description: methodology.json defines a 5-class claim taxonomy; claim_index.json applies it to 9 of 69+ claims as of 2026-04-18.
registry_level_gaps.why_not_per_win: The taxonomy-coverage gap is an audit over the registry's completeness, not per-WIN metadata. (Per-WIN metadata is the author_claim_class field from the schema extension.)
registry_level_gaps.proposed_home: Part 4 new subsection — "Registry Framing Audit"; short taxonomy callout+link also lives in Timestamp Error tab (because methodology.json is the author's formal methodology document and Timestamp Error is about methodology).
registry_level_gaps.testable: If classified_count grows toward 69 over time, taxonomy is real audit; if stalls near 9, taxonomy is a definition dressed as rigor.

registry_level_gaps.description: Three different layers of the author's site disagree on prospective_confirmed count: ai_manifest.json counts=10, visible grid shows 9, strict_prospective_confirmed in claim_index.json counts=1. (Newly-discovered instance, not previously tracked.)
registry_level_gaps.why_not_per_win: Count disagreement across three publications of the same aggregate.
registry_level_gaps.proposed_home: Part 4 new subsection — "Registry Framing Audit". Recommend opening new issue ISS-NEXT to track.

registry_level_gaps.description: live.html page footer reads "67 confirmed"; homepage reads "69 confirmed".

ai_manifest.json registry_semantics: "display-level bookkeeping". Visible site: pass/fail badges, 47.69σ tombstones, 94.5% accuracy headline. "Display-level bookkeeping" is a machine-readable disclaimer; the visible layer does not self-describe as such.
registry_level_gaps.why_not_per_win: Framing-level concession about the registry as a whole.

registry_level_gaps.description: ai_manifest.json / methodology.json: CVaR-based registry_exclusion_cap=6 (author's own conservative rule). Visible site: honors-system caveat is present in honest-caveats section but NOT at the point of emphasis (headline, scorecard, 94.5% tombstone).
registry_level_gaps.proposed_home: Part 4 new subsection — "Registry Framing Audit" (cross-references honest-caveats).

timestamp_tab_callout_html: Related: methodology.json 5-class claim taxonomy. Alongside the OTS timestamping methodology, the April 17 rigor push introduced a 5-class claim taxonomy (retrospective_structural, pending_contested, supportive_nonunique, prospective_confirmed, open_refinement) in methodology.json, applied to 9 of 69+ claims in claim_index.json. For the registry-level audit of how that taxonomy and other API-level concessions compare to the visible headline, see Section 4.2 — Registry Framing Audit. For per-WIN author-rating fields, see the Author's Self-Rating block on each WIN detail card.
previous_replacement_html_reason: User feedback 2026-04-18: Option B overloaded Timestamp Error tab scope. Replaced with three-part placement.
